titleEn: Tropical greenhouse strategies improve consistency under Thailand's challenging climate
titleZh: 热带温室策略助力泰国恶劣气候下番茄生产稳定性提升
summaryZh: 本文介绍了泰国番茄种植企业Take Me Home Tomato在热带低地气候下，通过嫁接环境控制、LED补光、基质更换（从椰糠转为岩棉）以及外部遮阳等综合策略，成功应对高温高湿挑战，实现产量稳定、品质提升和提前采收。文章还强调了数据驱动和精准灌溉对热带温室生产的重要性，为东南亚地区温室种植者提供了可借鉴的实践方案。
translationZh:
在热带低地气候下管理温室番茄生产，需要结合环境控制、一致的作物规程和数据驱动的决策。据Take Me Home Tomato公司首席执行官兼创始人Menno Keppel介绍，通过调整育苗和温室管理实践，该公司在泰国雨季前气温经常超过36°C的情况下仍能维持生产。

“嫁接本身在受控房间内进行，但恢复阶段更具挑战性，”他解释道。“植株在湿度帐篷内恢复，温度可能超过40°C。关键在于知道何时打开帐篷引入新鲜空气，同时保持足够的湿度以促进成功愈合。”

尽管在这种条件下嫁接最初导致大量植株损失，但通过优化苗圃规程，一致性得到了改善。“嫁接本身只要操作干净就相当简单，但让植株在第一周存活下来才是困难的部分。”

**LED补光提升苗圃一致性**
最大的改进之一是在温室苗圃中引入辅助LED补光。Menno分享说，自然光的季节性差异此前导致植株生长不均匀，使得全年难以匹配砧木和接穗。

“我们的植株在温暖期生长更快，而冬季则产生较慢、较小的植株。添加飞利浦LED补光，即使强度相对较低，也带来了巨大改变。植株在弱光期徒长减少，嫁接后恢复更快，我们实现了约95%的成功率。”

该公司还调整了育苗规程，将打顶推迟到植株更健壮时进行。“我们发现稍晚打顶效果更好，可以根据品种生产两个或三个强壮的头。”

**基质选择支持提前采收**
Take Me Home Tomato还观察到，从椰糠基质转向Grodan岩棉后，生产效益显著。

“我们认为Grodan提供了更好的干湿回管理，并在植株需要时提供更一致的养分供应。虽然椰糠看起来成本较低，但使用Grodan我们看到了更好的整体效果。”

据Menno称，作物大约提前一周达到采收期，同时果实品质得到改善。“风味更好，因为我们可以维持更高且更稳定的EC值。使用GroSense还让我们获得了以前从未有过的数据，从而能够更精确地进行作物调控和植株管理。”

**为季节性极端气候改造温室**
为应对泰国长达六个月的雨季，需要在维持植株健康的同时平衡高温和高湿。

“首先要从良好的作物处理规程、卫生以及对害虫的零容忍态度开始，”Menno补充道。

该公司的OptiClima温室采用外部遮阳，可在不限制自然通风的情况下减少太阳辐射。“我们在上午和下午利用最大可用光照，但一旦温室温度超过29°C，我们就启用外部遮阳幕。由于通风口保持打开状态，自然通风得以继续，同时温室温度可在没有主动冷却的情况下降低5至9°C。”

对于苗圃生产，Menno建议在嫁接愈合期间增加遮阳并保持高湿度。“使用ReduSol或ReduHeat等产品，或安装遮阳设施。我们还在苗圃内部使用遮阳，并在愈合帐篷上方增加额外的遮阳布，同时通过湿润地面或使用雾化系统来提高湿度。”

**精准种植日益重要**
在整个东南亚，他看到种植者正从配备湿帘风机降温的隧道系统转向更适合热带条件的自然通风温室。

“越来越多的种植者采用外部遮阳和反射涂层，因为降低峰值光照强度能显著减少植株胁迫。同时，种植者越来越认识到高质量育苗材料的重要性，因为更好的幼苗意味着更好的作物表现。”

他认为精准灌溉和获取技术知识的机会将对生产力产生最大影响。

“最重要的步骤是收集数据，让种植者了解温室内正在发生什么。结合专业知识，精准灌溉为提高生产力提供了最大机会，因为种植者可以精确供应作物所需，而不是依赖猜测。”
featuredReason: 本文聚焦热带地区温室番茄生产面临的真实挑战（高温、高湿、光照不均），并提供了从嫁接、补光、基质选择到遮阳策略的一整套可落地的解决方案。案例来自实际企业，数据详实（如95%嫁接成功率、提前一周采收、降温5-9°C），对东南亚及类似气候区的智慧园艺从业者具有直接参考价值。文章结构清晰、语言平实，兼具技术深度与可读性。

 Tropical greenhouse strategies improve consistency under Thailand's challenging climate

 

 
 Managing greenhouse tomato production in tropical lowland climates requires a combination of environmental control, consistent crop protocols, and data-driven decision-making. According to Menno Keppel, CEO and Founder of Take Me Home Tomato, adapting propagation and greenhouse management practices has enabled the company to maintain production despite temperatures regularly exceeding 36°C before Thailand's rainy season.

 "Grafting itself happens in a controlled room, but the recovery phase is much more challenging," he explains. "The plants recover inside humidity tents where temperatures can exceed 40°C. The key is knowing when to open the tents to bring in fresh air while maintaining enough humidity for successful healing."

 Although grafting under these conditions initially resulted in significant plant losses, refining nursery protocols has improved consistency. "The grafting itself is quite straightforward if you work clean, but keeping the plants alive during the first week is the difficult part."

 © Take Me Home Tomatoes

 LED lighting improves nursery consistencyOne of the biggest improvements came from introducing supplemental LED lighting in the greenhouse nursery. Menno shares that seasonal differences in natural light previously resulted in uneven plant growth, making it difficult to match rootstocks and scions throughout the year.

 © Take Me Home Tomatoes"Our plants grow much faster during warmer periods, while winter produces slower and smaller plants. Adding Philips LED lighting, even at relatively low levels, was a game changer. Plants stretched less during low-light periods, recovered faster after grafting, and we achieved around a 95% success rate."

 The company also adjusted its propagation protocol by delaying topping until plants are more established. "We found better results by topping slightly later, allowing us to produce two or three strong heads depending on the variety."

 Substrate choice supports earlier harvestsTake Me Home Tomato has also observed production benefits after moving from coco substrates to Grodan stone wool.

 "We believe Grodan provides better dry-back management and more consistent nutrient availability when the plant needs it. Although coco can appear less expensive, we see better overall results with Grodan."

 According to Menno, the crop reaches harvest approximately one week earlier while maintaining improved fruit quality. "Flavour is better because we can maintain a higher and more consistent EC. Using GroSense also gives us data we never had before, allowing more precise crop steering and plant manipulation."

 © Take Me Home Tomatoes

 Adapting greenhouses for seasonal extremesPreparing for Thailand's six-month rainy season requires balancing high temperatures with increasing humidity while maintaining plant health.

 "It starts with good protocols for crop handling, hygiene and a zero-tolerance approach to pests," Menno adds.

 © Take Me Home TomatoesThe company's OptiClima greenhouses use external shading that reduces solar radiation without restricting natural ventilation. "We use the maximum available light during the morning and afternoon, but once greenhouse temperatures exceed 29°C we deploy the outside screens. Because the vents remain open, natural ventilation continues while greenhouse temperatures can be reduced by 5 to 9°C without active cooling."

 For nursery production, Menno recommends additional shading together with high humidity during graft healing. "Use products such as ReduSol or ReduHeat, or install shading. We also use internal nursery shading and extra shade cloth above the healing tents, while increasing humidity by wetting the floor or using fogging."

 Precision growing becomes increasingly importantAcross Southeast Asia, he sees growers moving away from tunnel systems with pad-and-fan cooling towards naturally ventilated greenhouses that are better suited to tropical conditions.

 "More growers are adopting external shading and reflective coatings because reducing peak light intensity significantly lowers plant stress. At the same time, growers increasingly recognise the value of high-quality propagation material because better young plants translate into better crop performance."

 He believes precision irrigation and improved access to technical knowledge will have the greatest influence on productivity.

 "The most important step is collecting data, so growers understand what is happening inside the greenhouse. Combined with know-how, precision irrigation gives the greatest opportunity to improve productivity because growers can supply exactly what the crop needs instead of relying on guesswork."

 For more information:Take Me Home TomatoMenno Keppel[email protected] https://takemehometomato.com/

 GrodanT +31 47 53 53 535www.grodan.com

 SignifyDaniele Damoiseaux, Global Marcom Manager Horticulture [email protected]www.philips.com/horti
